summary: I worked as a longshoreman in Eureka, California, which is the county seat of Humboldt County. The Port of Eureka was where I began my waterfront career in 1966, and it was a place full of hardworking people and union activity. I remember the local union there, Local 14, and how the community was tightly knit around the waterfront industry.

Humboldt County, with Eureka as its county seat, was where my family settled after moving to California. I attended Arcata High School in Humboldt County starting in 1952, which helped me adjust to American life and learn English. The county was a significant part of my life as I began working in the lumber and sawmill industries before moving onto the waterfront.

I was born in France, in a small village called Saint-Sylvestre, and lived there until 1941 before moving to Gap in the French Alps. My family’s roots go deep in France, with my father tracing our lineage back to 1592. Growing up in France during World War II shaped much of my early life before we immigrated to the United States in 1951.

Saint-Sylvestre was my birthplace, a small village in Haute-Vienne, France, where I lived until 1941. It was a peaceful place before my family moved to the French Alps due to the war and economic reasons. Though I left at a young age, it remains an important part of my family history.

My parents were both born near Gap in the French Alps, and my family moved there in 1941 during the war. I spent a decade in Gap, a small city in the High Alps, which is also known as the door to Provence. The French Alps were a beautiful and formative place for me, and I have visited Gap many times since then.

Gap is where I lived from 1941 to 1951, a city in the Hautes-Alpes with about 10,000 people back then. It was a wonderful place to grow up, located about an hour and a half from the Mediterranean Sea. Gap is also notable for being a stop in the Tour de France, which I have followed closely over the years.

The Tour de France is a famous cycling race that recently stopped in Gap, the city where I spent much of my youth. It’s a beautiful event that highlights the region’s stunning landscape in the French Alps. Though I was not directly involved, the Tour de France connects me to my roots in Gap and the surrounding area.

The Mediterranean Sea is about an hour and a half drive from Gap, where I lived as a child. It represents the southern border of the region known as Provence, which Gap is considered the door to. The proximity to the sea added to the beauty and appeal of the area during my childhood.
cues: hour and a half from Gap

summary: World War II had a profound impact on my family and my early life. In 1941, during the German occupation of France, my father was drafted and sent away, so I didn’t see him much during that time. The war influenced our move within France and eventually led to our immigration to the United States in 1951.

My family moved to the United States in 1951 after growing disenchanted with the bureaucracy in post-war France. We first lived in Big Piney, Wyoming, then Pocatello, Idaho, before settling in California. The U.S. became my new home where I adapted to a new culture, learned English, and eventually built my career as a longshoreman.

After arriving in the United States, my family lived briefly in Pocatello, Idaho, for about six months. My father worked on the railroad there, but the opportunities were limited. Idaho was part of our early struggles as immigrants before we moved to California for better prospects.

Before returning to France, my father worked in Utah for French farmers during the 1920s and early 1930s. This experience gave him familiarity with the United States, which later influenced our decision to immigrate there in 1951. Utah was part of my father’s early working life abroad.

Our family history in the United States began in Colorado in the 1880s, where a great uncle worked in the mines. This was the start of the Ollivier family's immigration story to America. Colorado represents the early roots of our family in the U.S., long before I was born.

When we first arrived in the United States in 1951, we lived in Big Piney, Wyoming. It was a harsh environment with cold weather and little work, which made life difficult for my father and family. We didn’t stay there long before moving on to Idaho and then California.

After leaving Wyoming, we moved to Pocatello, Idaho, where my father found work on the railroad. We stayed there for about six months, but the opportunities were still limited. This was a transitional period before settling in California where we had family support.

California became our final destination after moving around the western U.S. My uncle Armand lived there, and it offered better work opportunities for my father and me. I attended Arcata High School in California and later worked in the lumber and plywood mills before joining the longshoremen in Eureka.

San Francisco was home to my uncle Armand and a place tied to my family’s history. My aunt Suzanne worked for the Folger coffee family there, and my uncle was involved in the waterfront scene, working in the Embarcadero area. The city also connects to the longshoremen’s strike of 1934 and Harry Bridges, whom my uncle respected greatly.

Fieldbrook was where my uncle Armand lived with his wife Suzanne and my uncle Léon after they bought property there in 1945. It was part of our family’s California roots and a place I visited when I first met my uncle in 1944. Fieldbrook represents the family’s settlement in Northern California.

My uncle Armand served in the French Army during World War II before also being drafted into the United States Army. This dual service reflects the complex history of our family during the war years. His military experience was part of the family stories I grew up hearing.

My uncle Armand was drafted into the United States Army during World War II after serving in the French Army. This shows his commitment to both his native and adopted countries during a difficult time. His military service was a significant part of his life and our family history.

My aunt Suzanne worked for the Folger family in San Francisco, known for their coffee business. She was one of the world’s best cooks, which made her a valued employee. Folgers represents a connection between our family and San Francisco’s working class and business community.

The Embarcadero in San Francisco was where my uncle Armand worked in a small cafe during the 1930s. It was also the site of the tragic killing of two longshoremen in 1934, events that led to the famous longshoremen’s strike. This area is deeply tied to my family’s connection to the waterfront and union history.

Harry Bridges was a legendary union leader whom my uncle Armand greatly respected. Though my uncle never met him personally, he followed his leadership during the formation of the International Longshore and Warehouse Union. When I was about to become a longshoreman, my uncle encouraged me by praising Bridges as one of the finest union leaders ever.

The longshoremen’s strike of 1934 was a pivotal event in San Francisco’s labor history, sparked by the killing of two longshoremen at the Embarcadero. My uncle Armand was working nearby during this time and witnessed the tensions firsthand. This strike helped shape the union movement that I later joined in Eureka.

After moving to California, I attended Arcata High School starting in 1952. This was where I learned English and studied American history and government, helping me adjust to life in the United States. Arcata was my first American school experience and a stepping stone to my future work life.

My first job in the United States was working as a bag boy at a Purity store, a grocery chain. I earned about a dollar an hour and stayed there for a few months before moving on to work in a sawmill. This job was part of my early American work experience as a teenager.

entity: Lumber and Sawmill Workers' Union, Local 2789
summary: I worked in a unionized plywood mill starting in 1953, which was part of the Lumber and Sawmill Workers' Union, Local 2789. This was my introduction to union work and hard labor, building my strength on the green chain. The union experience there helped prepare me for my later career as a longshoreman.

I joined the International Longshore and Warehouse Union in 1966 when I started working on the waterfront in Eureka. The union was a diverse group with Greeks, blacks, Swedes, Norwegians, and me, representing the French. The ILWU was a major part of my life for 35 years, and I loved every minute of working as a longshoreman under its banner.

The Port of Eureka was where I began my longshoreman career in 1966. It was a busy place with a strong union presence, and I quickly proved myself as a skilled lift driver. The port and its workers became my second family and the center of my working life for decades.

Local 14 was the union local in Eureka where I was interviewed and accepted as a longshoreman in 1966. It had about 140 members at the time, many of whom were tough, experienced gang bosses. Joining Local 14 was a proud moment for me, and it marked the beginning of a rewarding career on the waterfront.

Portland, Oregon was mentioned as part of the broader longshoremen’s union network, but I did not have direct personal experience there. It represents one of the other West Coast ports connected through the ILWU. My focus remained primarily on Eureka and Northern California.

Hilton Hotels & Resorts was not directly mentioned in my story or experiences. My work and family history were more connected to the waterfront, lumber mills, and union activities rather than the hotel industry.
